perturbation.original_text: 2. Use of Confidential Information; Confidentiality ​ . (a) Each Receiving Party shall keep all Confidential Information it receives or obtains from a Disclosing Party confidential and shall not, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, disclose such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, and shall not use such Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, for any purpose other than in connection with evaluating the Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Use of Confidential Information; Confidentiality ​ . (a) Each Receiving Party shall keep all Confidential Information it receives or obtains from a Disclosing Party confidential and shall not, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, disclose such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, and may use such Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, for any purpose in connection with evaluating the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'shall not use' to 'may use', removing the original restriction regarding the use of confidential information solely for evaluating the transaction. This contradicts the intent of Section 5, 'No Grant of Rights,' which states that no right is conveyed except the limited right to use Confidential Information described in Sections 1 and 2. Changing this section would contradict the specific use-right mentioned in Section 5. The removal of the restriction in Section 2 creates uncertainty in enforcing limitations on information use.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. No Grant of Rights ​ . No license, intellectual property right or other ownership or use right is conveyed solely by this Agreement, except the limited right to use Confidential Information described in Sections 1 and 2 of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 2(a) restricted the use of Confidential Information to solely evaluating the transaction ('shall not use...for any purpose other than in connection with evaluating the Transaction'). The changed text removes this restriction ('may use...for any purpose in connection with evaluating the Transaction'). This directly contradicts Section 5, which grants only a 'limited right to use Confidential Information described in Sections 1 and 2.' By removing the restriction in Section 2(a), the changed text expands the permitted use of confidential information beyond the limited right granted in Section 5, creating a clear in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15. Term and Termination ​ . This Agreement shall be in effect for a period of two (2) years from the date hereof, provided that (i) either Party may terminate this Agreement by giving 60 days’ written notice in accordance with the notice provisions hereof and (ii) with respect to Confidential Information disclosed hereunder, the confidentiality obligations set forth in Paragraph 2(a) shall remain in full force and effect for so long as such Confidential Information remains confidential and non-public.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. Term and Termination ​ . This Agreement shall be in effect for a period of two (2) years from the date hereof, provided that (i) either Party may terminate this Agreement by giving 60 days’ written notice in accordance with the notice provisions hereof and (ii) with respect to Confidential Information disclosed hereunder, the confidentiality obligations set forth in Paragraph 2(a) shall remain in full force and effect for a period of one (1) year after termination.
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the duration of the confidentiality obligations post-termination from 'so long as such Confidential Information remains confidential and non-public' to a fixed period of one year. This conflicts with Section 2(a), which originally imposed confidentiality obligations without a fixed end date as long as the information remains confidential. This in-text contradiction creates uncertainty about how long confidential information is protected, impacting enforcement and legal obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Use of Confidential Information; Confidentiality ​ . (a) Each Receiving Party shall keep all Confidential Information it receives or obtains from a Disclosing Party confidential and shall not, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, disclose such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, and shall not use such Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, for any purpose other than in connection with evaluating the Transaction. The exposure of the Receiving Party to the Confidential Information or its disclosure shall not be construed in any way to grant to the Receiving Party any right or license with respect to the Information other than the right to use such Confidential Information strictly in accordance with this Agreement. Moreover, each Receiving Party shall have the right Confidentiality Non-Disclosure Agreement 9 Page 2 of 6 Rev. 08-Oct-18 to disclose that it is evaluating the Transaction and transmit Confidential Information to its Representatives only if and to the extent that such Representatives need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of evaluating the Transaction and are informed by such Receiving Party of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and agree (in writing) to be bound by the terms of this Agreement. Each Party shall be responsible for any actions by its Affiliates and/or Representatives which are not in accordance with the agreements herein regarding the conduct of its Affiliates and/or Representatives. Visitors shall not take photographs or otherwise record the activities at the other party’s facilities.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 15 to limit the confidentiality obligations in Paragraph 2(a) to a fixed period of one year after termination. The original text of Section 15, and Section 2(a) impose the confidentiality obligations for 'so long as such Confidential Information remains confidential and non-public'. The change introduces a direct conflict by contradicting the indefinite duration of confidentiality with a fixed one-year term, leading to uncertainty and inconsistency within the contract, thus creating an in-text contradiction as described in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Indemnification ​ . In the event any Representative of a Party is permitted at any time to visit any of the business or project sites of another Party or any of its Affiliates, such visiting Party shall indemnify and save such other Party harmless from and against any and all claims and liabilities, including actual out-of-pocket costs and expenses for loss, injury to or death of any such person, and any loss, damage to or destruction of any property, incurred by such other Party or any of its Affiliates resulting directly or indirectly from the negligence or misconduct of any of such Representatives during any such visit. In no event shall the indemnifying Party be liable for consequential damages.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Indemnification ​ . In the event any Representative of a Party is permitted at any time to visit any of the business or project sites of another Party or any of its Affiliates, such visiting Party shall indemnify and save such other Party harmless from and against any and all claims and liabilities, including actual out-of-pocket costs and expenses for loss, injury to or death of any such person, and any loss, damage to or destruction of any property, incurred by such other Party or any of its Affiliates resulting directly or indirectly from the negligence or misconduct of any of such Representatives during any such visit. In no event shall the indemnifying Party's liability be capped or limited, including consequential damages.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'In no event shall the indemnifying Party be liable for consequential damages' to 'In no event shall the indemnifying Party's liability be capped or limited, including consequential damages'. This creates a direct conflict and an in-text contradiction because the original clause explicitly excludes consequential damages from indemnification, while the modified clause states that liability cannot be capped or limited and includes consequential damages. This contradiction causes uncertainty as it's unclear whether consequential damages are recoverable under the agreement, leading to potential disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Indemnification ​ . In the event any Representative of a Party is permitted at any time to visit any of the business or project sites of another Party or any of its Affiliates, such visiting Party shall indemnify and save such other Party harmless from and against any and all claims and liabilities, including actual out-of-pocket costs and expenses for loss, injury to or death of any such person, and any loss, damage to or destruction of any property, incurred by such other Party or any of its Affiliates resulting directly or indirectly from the negligence or misconduct of any of such Representatives during any such visit. In no event shall the indemnifying Party's liability be capped or limited, including consequential damages.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 3 to remove the exclusion of consequential damages from indemnification and explicitly states that liability cannot be capped or limited, including consequential damages. This directly contradicts the original intent and introduces an inconsistency within the same section (3) of the contract. The original text in section 3 explicitly excludes consequential damages, while the changed text in the same section includes it, creating a direct conflict regarding whether consequential damages are recoverable. Therefore, this is a clear and impactful contradiction as described in the Perturbation Explanation, falling under 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.
